title: How Many Components Should You Keep?
concept: Choosing how many principal components to keep is a tradeoff: keep enough to preserve most of the variance (often around 95%) while dropping the rest to simplify.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How are principal components ranked?

options.text: By how much variance each one explains, from most (PC1) to least
By how many original columns they include

exercises.explain: Right! Components are ranked from most variance explained to least. Next question: how many should you actually keep?

cards.title: Why phone charging slows down near 100%
cards.body: Charging from 0% to 80% is fast — you gain most of the battery's usable capacity quickly. But squeezing out that last 20%, from 80% to 100%, takes almost as long as the first 80% did. The big wins come first, and the last little bit costs a lot for not much extra gain.

cards.title: Choosing how many components to keep
cards.body: Each principal component explains a certain percentage of the data's total variance. Add up the percentages from PC1, PC2, PC3... and you get the cumulative variance explained. A common rule of thumb: keep adding components only until that running total reaches a target — often around 95% — then stop and drop the rest.

cards.title: Wringing out a wet towel
cards.body: The first twist of a soaking towel wrings out most of the water. Twisting it again and again after that only squeezes out a few more drops for a lot more effort. Principal components work the same way: the first few wring out most of the useful variance, and the later ones barely add anything for the extra complexity they cost.

cards.title: A real PCA breakdown
cards.body: A dataset has 10 features. After PCA, PC1 explains 45% of the variance, PC2 explains 25%, PC3 explains 15%, PC4 explains 8%, and the remaining 6 components add up to only 7% combined. Keeping just PC1–PC3 already preserves 85% of the picture, and adding PC4 pushes it to 93% — while the last 6 components barely move the needle.

cards.title: More components isn't automatically better
cards.body: It's tempting to think you should always keep as many components as possible so you don't lose any information. In reality, keeping every component defeats the purpose of dimensionality reduction — the last few components typically capture noise more than signal, so trimming them usually makes models faster and sometimes even more accurate.

cards.title: Few components vs. many components
cards.body: The tradeoff when choosing how many to keep:

left.text:
Simple, fast
Risk: might lose some real signal

right.text:
Preserves more detail
Risk: keeps noise, less simplification

exercises.prompt: What does "cumulative variance explained" mean when choosing how many components to keep?

options.text: The running total of variance captured by PC1, PC2, PC3... added together
The variance of just the very last component
The number of original features in the dataset

exercises.explain: Right! Cumulative variance explained is the running total as you add more and more components.

exercises.prompt: True or false: You should always keep every principal component to avoid losing any information.
exercises.explain: False! Keeping every component defeats the purpose of dimensionality reduction — the last few usually add mostly noise, not signal.

exercises.sentence: A common rule of thumb is to keep adding components until the ___ variance explained reaches around 95%.
exercises.explain: The cumulative (running total) variance explained is what you track to decide how many components to keep.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario shows sensible component selection?

options.text: A team keeps the first 6 of 50 components because together they already explain 96% of the variance, and stops there
A team keeps all 50 components "just in case"
A team keeps only PC1 with no regard for how much total variance is actually preserved

exercises.explain: Correct! Stopping once the cumulative variance explained hits a solid target (like 96%) is exactly the tradeoff PCA component selection is built around.

exercises.prompt: A dataset has 20 features. PCA shows PC1–PC5 together explain 94% of the variance, while PC6–PC20 combined add only 6% more. A teammate wants to keep all 20 components "just in case." What's the strongest argument for keeping only the first 5?

options.text: The first 5 already preserve nearly all the meaningful variance, while the remaining 15 add little signal but plenty of extra complexity and likely noise
PCA requires you to always keep exactly 5 components
Components after PC1 contain no useful information at all
Keeping more components always makes a model perform worse

exercises.explain: Exactly! When a small number of components already capture the vast majority of the variance, keeping the rest mostly adds noise and complexity for very little extra signal.
